title: El mundo de atrás
narrative:
Short impact summary

Claudio Canaparo's El mundo de atrás, published by Peter Lang Verlag in 2019, occupies a niche but distinctive position at the intersection of Latin American philosophy, human ecology, and the humanities-science dialogue. Formal citation uptake remains minimal at this stage, and the book sits in the lowest citation percentiles for its cohort, yet it has secured a presence in the commercial book ecosystem and holds library cataloguing across multiple institutions.

Full narrative

The most consistently traceable signal for El mundo de atrás is its commercial visibility. The book is indexed on both Amazon and Goodreads, a combination that extends its discoverability well beyond the conventional scholarly circuit and places it in front of general readers with interests in Latin American thought, environmental philosophy, and the broader relationship between science and the humanities. This kind of retail and reader-platform presence, while not a substitute for formal academic uptake, suggests that Peter Lang Verlag positioned the title for a wider audience than specialist monographs typically reach.

Library holdings, though modest at four confirmed copies tracked through Open Library, carry meaningful qualitative weight. WorldCat subject classifications assign the book to environmental sciences, science and the humanities, human ecology, and Latin American philosophy — a cross-disciplinary clustering that reflects the work's thematic ambition and may support its discovery by researchers approaching these questions from quite different disciplinary angles. This breadth of subject tagging can, over time, broaden the pool of scholars who encounter the title through catalogue searches.

Formal citation evidence is currently absent from indexed sources, and the book's percentile ranking places it at the very low end of measured scholarly impact for its publication year and type. This is not unusual for single-authored philosophical monographs published in a language other than English, particularly in the early years following publication; such works often accumulate citations slowly and through disciplinary channels that are themselves underrepresented in mainstream citation databases. The absence of recorded citations should therefore be read as a data limitation as much as a substantive finding.

Taken together, the available evidence positions El mundo de atrás as a work whose influence, if it develops, is likely to do so gradually and within specialist communities focused on Latin American intellectual history, environmental humanities, and philosophy of science. Its commercial linkages provide a durable access point, and its cross-disciplinary library classification keeps it available to a variety of scholarly audiences. Continued monitoring of citation activity and potential syllabi adoption would be the clearest indicators of whether the book's thematic scope translates into sustained academic engagement.
